sentences.text: And the second I'm going to spell you.
We lived-- we lived together, my parents and three brothers.
The one is-- The oldest was David.
I am the middle one, and the youngest was Michael.
For example, 80% of our neighbors, they were Polish.
I remember my nearest neighbor was named Kowal, like K-O-W-A-L.
You want to know more about my neighbor?
One of the son was with British army.
And he went to-- after the war to Scotland.
Unfortunately he died two years ago.
And he got one son what living in Dumfries in Scotland.
My friends, 80%, my friends, they were Polish people.
Generally, there were anti-semites in Poland, but not in our area.
The first school, this was normal school.
And after, I went to gymnasium.
And I went five years to gymnasium.
One was Balala, and another, I don't remember.
But in gymnasium, I remember their name, like Professor Harasek, Professor Krauzow, what was a woman.
The same-- it was in this school was 80% Polish people, and 20%, maybe 10% Jews.
I could remember a few, just few.
For example, Bieberstein, Makari, Wengzynowicz, Koscka, Myzczynski.
Maybe I-- it is enough for me.
From '33, when Hitler came to power, there was very heavy propaganda from Germany against the Jews.
And they came to Poland, too.
And this time I was in gymnasium, and they start to be anti-semite.
So I get a lot of trouble.
And it was so far that we were fighting together.
And they called my father and told him that I should go from gymnasium, because otherwise, they want to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] expel me.
So my father, he would not-- didn't see another way.
He took me out from gymnasium, and I started to do be-- because I wanted to go to Palestine, I wanted to be in trade.
So I went to learn painting and decorating.
What type of-- going back a little bit then, the anti-semitism that you had, what type, what did your friends do to you or say?
They-- they start to beat the Jews on the street and demonstrated against them.
And because of them, I went out from gymnasium.
Because I didn't see any future in Poland.
In Germany or abroad were impossible.
We didn't even-- we didn't even dream to go abroad.
Just the purpose to go was one country, was Palestine.
And generation, generation they were still in Poland.
So they didn't think about just to leave the Poland.
My father was in transport trade.
This time, the women didn't work.
We been-- my father was very conservative-- how would you-- liberal conservative.
So we just-- he went Saturday to the synagogue.
I remember, it was-- I don't know how to-- to say in English.
But I didn't like-- I didn't like to go there.
Because the younger-- the young generation, it was modern.
And they start to go to dancing, and with girls, and smoking cigarettes.
It was-- the Jewish-English say a shul.
You notice differences, are very small, though [NON-ENGLISH] people.
But there were-- for me, it was too many-- too many synagogues.
I don't know, but too many for me.
And tradition like Yom Kippur and [NON-ENGLISH] were they keeping.
Until today, I know exactly the [NON-ENGLISH] what I made.
But how many people, I don't remember.
As you know what is it-- kiddush.
Like with my mother, I was like friend.
And my father was a little bit [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] distance.
But I haven't got to be contact to him.
Because this time-- this time the people, as father and son, it was something too many, they wanted too many respect.
They were like-- like brothers, oh We always have been like brothers.
We've been all three in Jewish Zionist organization.
We were together almost every evening to learn Hebrew, and and another of Zionists, and [HEBREW].
In Akiba, we've been just young people.
But I-- when I was-- I was 18, I went to older people, or the organization [HEBREW].
And we start already to smoke cigars, and dancing, and flirting, and--
They killed her in the war.
In Cracow, because we should leave Cracow.
She went with her parents to a one village outside Cracow, and we went with my parents to another village.
So we didn't see each other anymore.
We had been forced to leave.
Just as people stay in ghetto, or they get permission from the German.
And the German pick up these people what was necessary for them to make the arms of the industry.
After, when they concentrate-- this was '42.
They concentrate people in Cracow ghetto.
And from Cracow ghetto, they sent groups of thousands of thousands of people to gas chamber like Belzec, or Auschwitz.
I did not answer that question.
From my friend, Polish, very good friend, we start to be little bit distance.
But I blame maybe the Polish church, and the rest German propaganda.
Because the Polish church, they were always anti-semite.
Official, we had the same right, The Jews, the same right like the Polish.
For example, if somebody from Jewish people wanted to study medicine, is well not possible.
Because they made a law, what is numerus clausus.
What they mean, limited-- limited number of Jewish students could study medicine.
So most of Jews, they went to Prague, to the Czechoslovakia, and Italy, even to England.
I went to a master what was painting and decorating.
Yes, my first master what I was learning, was Weinreb.
I can spell to you Weinreb.
Even though I went-- after the-- when I was already skilled, I went to a master, Polish master, was very good to me.
You want to have to spell?
Because until today, I think I was good painter.
All together as a painter and decorator?
I was working in another Jewish master.
And we had been working for a very known optician doctor.
And this optician doctor was married, Polish.
He was-- had been married with a Jewish woman.
Because of that, when that Germans came, they took his flat, what was 10 rooms.
And in this flat was a German general.
And when the German came, the house, it wasn't ready.
So the German force us to carry on until the house is going to be finished.
So we-- I was-- I was in this house for the general-- painting.
Because this time they already force us to work.
Carry on the David star, you know.
And it was not very pleasant.
And his boss, he was a Polish officer what was Jewish.
And they-- the German took him as prisoner of war.
And my brother was the manager after the German give him another boss.
They took away the pharmacy from the Jews.
He learned because he want to go to Palestine.
But '38, he wasn't finished with his-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with his learning.
So he went to Russia because he don't like-- he didn't like to be with German.
In Russia, the people told me, came with the return from Russia, he told me that they met him.
And he voluntary went to Polish army in Russia to fight the German.
In '41, when the German attacked Russia, he-- the Polish army went against Germany, And he fell.
My parents, this was not-- the men, they were forced to clean the street, and ordinary work to do without salary.
Before-- until '40, we have been nowhere in Cracow.
Yes. '40, we went to the village.
And the peasant around, they start to ask what I'm doing.
And suddenly it was occurred to me, because I was very busy every day, wanted to-- from Cracow a painter, it was something special for the peasant.
So-- but the salary, I didn't take money.
I was living with my parents.
This is like 10 miles from Cracow.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That is what-- June.
My brother was in Cracow, in ghetto.
After this, '41, we got an order from German to leave the village.
We didn't know why, but they concentrate the Jews in towns and small towns.
So the Jews from the villages, they should go-- for example, to Skawina, what was very small town, they concentrate the Jews in Skawina.
And my parents and my relation, what we have been all together in this village, Zielonki, they went to Skawina.
And my girlfriend with parents also in in Zielonki.
But we didn't want to be split.
No official, because the official was not allowed.
So just according to the Jewish law, [HEBREW]
So anyway, we went another village, what was not far from Cracow.
And we'd been in this village until '42 June.
Very small. 90%, 95%, 98% population, they were Polish.
But '42, they would concentrate the outside Cracow people to ghetto Cracow.
But when we came to this ghetto, in two days, suddenly SS and gestapo surrounded ghetto.
And they took 5,000 or 6,000 people, and they sent them to the wagons.
But after a few weeks, we find that they went to-- this was-- this was village Belzec.
And they were built from of the German gas chamber.
So we knew that we going to that.
